revision.text:
[[en:Japanese Buddhist pantheon]][[fa:پانتئون بودایی ژاپن]][[fr:Panthéon bouddhiste japonais]][[id:Panteon Buddhis Jepang]][[ja:日本の仏尊]][[lt:Sąrašas:Budizmo mitologijos sąvokos]] {{Short description|Buddhist pantheon of Japan}} [[File:Buddhist Pantheon.jpg|thumb|450px|Some of the main representatives of the Buddhist pantheon, in [[:en:Musée Guimet|Musée Guimet]], Paris]] {{Japanese Buddhism}} The '''Japanese Buddhist pantheon''' designates the multitude (the [[:en:Pantheon (gods)|pantheon]]) of various [[:en:Buddhas|Buddhas]], {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} and lesser [[Kami|Deities]] and eminent religious masters in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}. A Buddhist Pantheon exists to a certain extent in [[:en:Mahāyāna|Mahāyāna]]. Still it is especially characteristic of {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} [[:en:Esoteric Buddhism|Esoteric Buddhism]], including {{ill|Tibetan Buddhism|en|Tibetan Buddhism|qid=Q483889|lt=Tibetan Buddhism|1=Tibetan Buddhism}} and especially Japanese {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}}, which formalized it to a great extent. In the ancient {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese Buddhist pantheon, more than 3,000 Buddhas or deities have been counted, although now most temples focus on one Buddha and a few Bodhisattvas.[https://books.google.com/books?id=YqgbJ0EmV4oC&pg=PA87 ''Religion of the Samurai'' Kaiten Nukariya p.87] ==History== [[Image:Mandala1 detail.jpg|thumb|A Japanese [[mandala]] of the Five [[:en:Dhyani|Dhyani]] Buddhas, and other {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}, surrounding the central Buddha {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}}.]] {{ill|Pre-sectarian Buddhism|en|Pre-sectarian Buddhism|qid=Q853482|lt=Pre-sectarian Buddhism|1=Pre-sectarian Buddhism}} had a somewhat vague position on the existence and effect of deities. Indeed, Buddhism is often considered atheistic on account of its denial of a creator god and human responsibility to it. However, nearly all modern Buddhist schools accept the existence of deities of some kind; the main point of divergence is the influence of these deities. Of the major schools, {{ill|Theravada|en|Theravada|qid=Q132265|lt=Theravada|1=Theravada}} tends to de-emphasize the deities, whereas {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}} and {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} do not. The rich Buddhist Pantheon of northern Buddhism ultimately derives from {{ill|Vajrayana|en|Vajrayana|qid=Q172175|lt=Vajrayana|1=Vajrayana}} and [[:en:Tantrism|Tantrism]].''An Introduction to Buddhist Esoterism'' Benoytosh Bhattacharyya p.120 [https://books.google.com/books?id=xJ3MqlIwL2UC&pg=PA120] The historical devotional roots of pantheistic Buddhism seem to go back to the period of the {{ill|Kushan Empire|en|Kushan Empire|qid=Q25979|lt=Kushan Empire|1=Kushan Empire}}.''Buddhist art & antiquities of Himachal Pradesh, up to 8th century A.D.'' Omacanda Hāṇḍā p.82 [https://books.google.com/books?id=6Cqgb9pL3L4C&pg=PA82] The first proper mention of a Buddhist Pantheon appears in the 3-4th century ''[[:en:Guhyasamāja|Guhyasamāja]]'', in which five Buddhas are mentioned, the emanations of which constitute a family:''An Introduction to Buddhist Esoterism'' Benoytosh Bhattacharyya p.121 [https://books.google.com/books?id=xJ3MqlIwL2UC&pg=PA121] {{quote|The five Kulas are Dvesa, Moha, Rāga, Cintāmani, and Samaya, which conduce to the attainment of all desires and emancipation|''Guhyasamāja''.}} By the 9th century under the {{ill|Pala Empire|en|Pala Empire|qid=Q668412|lt=Pala|1=Pala Empire}} king {{ill|Dharmapala of Bengal|en|Dharmapala of Bengal|qid=Q3183804|lt=Dharmapala|1=Dharmapala of Bengal}}, the Buddhist Pantheon had already swelled to about 1,000 Buddhas.''Buddhist art & antiquities of Himachal Pradesh, up to 8th century A.D.'' Omacanda Hāṇḍā p.83 [https://books.google.com/books?id=6Cqgb9pL3L4C&pg=PA83] In Japan, {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}} introduced [[Shingon]] [[:en:Esoteric Buddhism|Esoteric Buddhism]] and its Buddhist Pantheon, also in the 9th century.''The body: toward an Eastern mind-body theory'' Yasuo Yuasa, Thomas P. Kasulis p.125 [https://books.google.com/books?id=_DOku4pM5wYC&pg=PA125] ==Hierarchical structure== The Buddhist Pantheon in Japanese Buddhism is defined by a hierarchy in which the [[:en:Buddhas|Buddhas]] occupy the topmost Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category, followed in order by the numerous Bodhisattvas, the [[:en:Wisdom Kings|Wisdom Kings]], the Deities, the "Circumstantial appearances" and lastly the [[patriarch]]s and eminent religious people.Notice at [[:en:Musée Guimet|Musée Guimet]] {|class="wikitable" |- !Level !Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category !{{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|qid=Q5287|lt=Japanese nomenclature|1=Japanese language}} |- |Level 1 |{{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=Buddhas|1=Buddhahood}} |{{nihongo|''Nyorai-bu''|如来部}} |- |Level 2 |{{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}s |{{nihongo|''Bosatsu-bu''|菩薩部}} |- |Level 3 |[[Wisdom King]]s |{{nihongo|''Myōō-bu''|明王部}} |- |Level 4 |{{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Heavenly deities|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} |{{nihongo|''Ten-bu''|天部}} |- |Level 5 |[[Gongen|Circumstantial appearances]] |{{nihongo|Gongen|権現}} |- |Level 6 |{{ill|Bhikkhu|en|Bhikkhu|qid=Q854997|lt=Religious masters|1=Bhikkhu}} |{{nihongo|''Kōsō'' - ''Soshi''|高僧・祖師}} |} A famous statue group, the [[mandala]] located at [[Tō-ji]] temple in [[:en:Kyōto|Kyōto]], shows some of the main elements and structure of the Buddhist Pantheon. The mandala was made in the 9th century and offered to {{ill|Kūkai|en|Kūkai|qid=Q222227|lt=Kūkai|1=Kūkai}}.Musée Guimet exhibit A duplicate was brought to {{ill|Paris|en|Paris|qid=Q90|lt=Paris|1=Paris}}, {{ill|France|en|France|qid=Q142|lt=France|1=France}}, by [[:en:Emile Guimet|Emile Guimet]] at the end of the 19th century, and is now located in the [[:en:Musée Guimet|Musée Guimet]]. Japanese Buddhism incorporated numerous {{ill|List of Japanese deities|en|List of Japanese deities|qid=Q3240847|lt=Shintō deities|1=List of Japanese deities}} in its pantheon and reciprocally. Japanese Shingon also has other categories, such as the {{ill|Thirteen Buddhas|en|Thirteen Buddhas|qid=Q3538361|lt=Thirteen Buddhas|1=Thirteen Buddhas}}.[https://books.google.com/books?id=LMmX8OJ4RLUC&pg=PA338 ''Sources of Japanese tradition'' William Theodore De Bary, p.338] {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} however clearly rejected the strong polytheistic conceptions of orthodox Buddhism.[https://books.google.com/books?id=YqgbJ0EmV4oC&pg=PA87 ''Religion of the Samurai'' Kaiten Nukariya p.88] ===Level 1: Buddhas (Nyorai-bu)=== A Buddha is one who has attained enlightenment and reached the state of nirvana. Buddhas are distinct from Bodhisattvas because they have chosen to leave earth. [[File:Houshou.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Ratnasambhava|1=Ratnasambhava}}/ {{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Ratnasambhava|1=Ratnasambhava}} Buddha.]] ====Five Wisdom Buddhas==== {{main|Nyorai|Five Wisdom Buddhas}} The five Wisdom Buddhas (五仏) are centered around {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}} (Japanese: Dainichi Nyorai, 大日如来), the supreme Buddha. Each of the four remaining Buddhas occupies a fixed [[:en:Cardinal point|Cardinal point]]. Each of them is a manifestation of Buddhahood, and each is active in a different world-period, in which they manifest themselves among Bodhisattvas and humans.[https://books.google.com/books?id=de9euU8z8PgC&pg=PA22 ''Religion in Nepal'' by K. R. van Kooij p.22] An enlightened being is one who embodies the qualities of the five Buddha Families, or the five Wisdom Buddhas, and in doing so has shed the negative emotions which cause pain and suffering throughout life.{{Cite book|title=The five Buddha families & the eight consciousnesses|last=Thrangu, Rinpoche|date=2001|publisher=Zhyisil Chokyi GhatsalPublications|isbn=1877294144|oclc=155719092}} These five key emotions are known as “disturbing” emotions and they include: attachment, anger, ignorance, pride, and envy. When these emotions are exercised they cause ourselves and others around us harm and suffering and can potentially cause a lower level reincarnation in the next life. Therefore, by eliminating these emotions allow one to attain enlightenment by recognizing and becoming one with the five Wisdom Buddha. [[File:Five Wisdom Buddhas and four Bodhisattvas at the corners.jpg|thumb|left|The five Wisdom Buddhas, guarded by four Great Diamond Bodhisattvas at the corners. The Buddha at the front is at the South and is {{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Ratnasambhava|1=Ratnasambhava}}.]] {|border="0" cellspacing="5" | |align = "center"|[[Amoghasiddhi|Fukūjōju Nyorai]] (north) | |- |align = "center"|[[Amitābha|Amida Nyorai]] (west) |align = "center"|{{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|1=Vairocana}}
(principal deity) |align = "center"|[[Akshobhya|Ashuku Nyorai]] (east) |- | |align = "center"|{{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Hōshō Nyorai|1=Ratnasambhava}} (south) | |} These "[[:en:Dhyani Buddhas|Dhyani Buddhas]]" form the core of the Buddhist pantheistic system, which developed from them in a multiform way. At the Musée Guimet, the five Buddhas are surrounded by protective Bodhisattvas. The five Wisdom Buddhas are known as, Akshobhya, Ratnasambhava, Amitabha, Amogasiddha, and Vairocana. They each have varying characteristics and attributes specific to their purpose. The first Buddha, '''Akshobhya''', is colored blue and sits in a vajra posture with his hand touching the ground. The color blue and the vajra posture symbolize changelessness and permanance which is particular to him because he focuses on easing emotions that spur from anger. His wisdom is known as the “mirror-like” wisdom because when one is freed from anger and the feelings accompanied with anger, one is able to have an unbiased awareness of our daily experiences. "Mirror-like" wisdom is the idea that one can see things for how they really are instead of having a blurred perspective that is caused from one's anger getting in the way of seeing the truth. The second Buddha, '''Ratnasambhava''', is concerned with the enrichment of oneself. When one has been cleansed of the disturbing emotion of pride, one's ego becomes objective and this enables fairness and equality in regards to all aspects of one's life. This Buddha is a yellowish, gold color and he holds a wish-fulfilling jewel in his hand. The golden color is meant to symbolize wealth in a fulfilled sense and the wish-fulfilling jewel symbolizes his activity of enrichment because it is able to grant any desirable wish. This Buddha sits in vajra posture which represents fulfillment and suggests supreme generosity by giving the {{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=Mudra|1=Mudra}} hand gesture. The third Buddha, '''Amitabha''', is focused on the elimination of the strong feeling of desire. Desire is one of the five disturbing emotions that causes one to have neverending wants and ultimately cultivates suffering. If one cannot attain his desires then he will feel unfulfilled and empty. The loss of great desire allows one to rise above to a more simplistic way of life with overwhelming gratitude. With recognition of this Buddha one will be able to find appreciation in the small things and see things for their true worth with an unbiased perspective. Buddha Amitabha is from the lotus family and is seated in vajra posture with his hands placed in the meditative posture for mental clarity. The fourth Buddha, '''Amogasiddha''', is focused on the strength of wisdom and the elimination of jealousy. Jealousy is a hindrance in which infringes on and distracts from ones wisdomly abilities. Buddha Amogasiddha’s activity is “meaningful accomplishment” which spurs from undistracted and engaged wisdom. With great wisdom, one is able to observe and overcome trivial uprisings in an intelligent and calm manner. His right hand is gesturing "fearless protection" from things that may hinder one's wisdom. The fifth Buddha, '''Vairocana''', is focused on the elimination of ignorance from one's mental state. Ignorance makes one’s perspective unclear and causes one to make judgements from a subjective view. Buddha Vairocana holds the wheel of dharma, the symbol of buddhist law, in his hands. The gesture of the wheel of dharma serves the purpose of symbolizing uninterrupted knowledge of how the world works. The wheel represents knowledge of the Buddhas teachings which lead one to enlightenment. There is also a multitude of other Buddhas, such as {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, the Buddha of medicine. Buddha Yakushi was widely worshipped during the Heian Period in Japan.{{Citation|last=Suzuki|first=Yui|chapter=2: The Magical Yakushi: Spirit Pacifier and Healer-God|pages=29–44|publisher=Brill|isbn=9789004229174|doi=10.1163/9789004229174_004|title=Medicine Master Buddha: The Iconic Worship of Yakushi in Heian Japan|year=2012}} Yakushi was known as the [[:en:Yakushi|Medicine Buddha]] and people would pray to him for protection against vengeful spirits and natural disaster. Yakushi can be recognized in Buddhist art by the iconography of the medicine jar which he holds in his hand. ===Level 2: Bodhisattvas (Bosatsu-bu)=== {{main|Bodhisattvas}} [[File:Kongo Haramitsu Bodhisattva.jpg|thumb|Bodhisattva [[:en:Kongō-Haramitsu|Kongō-Haramitsu]]/ [[:en:Vajraparamita|Vajraparamita]].]] A bodhisattva is one who has attained enlightenment and has chosen to stay on earth and spread his knowledge of enlightenment to others so that they too can gain enlightenment.{{Cite journal|last1=Morse|first1=A. N.|last2=Morse|first2=S. C.|date=1995|title=Object as Insight: Japanese Buddhist Art and Ritual|url=https://learn-us-east-1-prod-fleet01-xythos.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/5af9c29bb5c60/625482?response-content-disposition=inline%3B%20filename%2A%3DUTF-8%27%27object-as-insight_introduction.pdf&response-content-type=application%2Fpdf&X-Amz-Algorithm=AWS4-HMAC-SHA256&X-Amz-Date=20190408T010038Z&X-Amz-SignedHeaders=host&X-Amz-Expires=21600&X-Amz-Credential=AKIAIBGJ7RCS23L3LEJQ%2F20190408%2Fus-east-1%2Fs3%2Faws4_request&X-Amz-Signature=2573cdf548e9b393db85bd581557210ba9e74b1b15457865f4ddb520fa307bdc|journal=Katonah Museum of Art}} Bodhisattvas are paragons of compassion in Mahayana Buddhism. In the Buddhist Pantheon, besides the past and future Buddhas, there are numerous Bodhisattvas as well.[https://books.google.com/books?id=I1uFuXlvFgMC&pg=PA129 ''The Hero with a Thousand Faces'' Joseph Campbell p.129] Sometimes, five main "Matrix" Bodhisattvas are determined (五大菩薩), grouped around a central Bodhisattva, [[:en:Kongō-Haramitsu|Kongō-Haramitsu]] (金剛波羅蜜菩薩) in the case of [[Tō-ji|Tōji]] Temple. [[File:Bodhisattvas Pantheon.jpg|thumb|left|Five Matrix Bodhisattvas, guarded front right by [[Four Heavenly Kings|Heavenly King]] {{ill|Dhṛtarāṣṭra|en|Dhṛtarāṣṭra|qid=Q1188767|lt=Dhṛtarāṣṭra|1=Dhṛtarāṣṭra}}, and back right Heavenly King {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Tamon|1=Vaiśravaṇa}}. [[:en:Musée Guimet|Musée Guimet]].]] {|border="0" cellspacing="5" | |align = "center"|[[Maitreya|Miroku]] (north) | |- |align = "center"|[[Kannon]] (west) |align = "center"|[[:en:Kongō-Haramitsu|Kongō-Haramitsu]]
(principal deity) |align = "center"|{{ill|Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)|en|Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)|qid=Q868306|lt=Fugen|1=Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)}} (east) |- | |align = "center"|[[:en:Manjusri|Monju]] (south) | |} Beyond these five main {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}, there exists a huge number of other Bodhisattvas, all beings who have postponed enlightenment for the benefit of helping mankind. File:Kongohou_Bosatsu_Vajraratsa.jpg|Kongōhō Bosatsu / Vajraratsa. File:Daizuigu Mahapratisara.jpg|[[:en:Daizuigu|Daizuigu]]. File:Jizo-osorezan-jpatokal.jpg|{{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Jizō|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. File:KOKYUZO Akasagarbha Shingoji.JPG|{{ill|Ākāśagarbha|en|Ākāśagarbha|qid=Q416174|lt=Kokūzō|1=Ākāśagarbha}}. File:Chinese temple bouddha.jpg|{{ill|Mahasthamaprapta|en|Mahasthamaprapta|qid=Q1465683|lt=Seishi|1=Mahasthamaprapta}}. ===Level 3: Wisdom Kings (Myōō-bu)=== {{main|Wisdom Kings}} [[File:Gundari.jpg|thumb|The [[Wisdom King]] [[:en:Gundari|Gundari]] is a manifestation of one of the Five Buddhas, {{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Ratnasambhava|1=Ratnasambhava}}/ {{ill|Ratnasambhava|en|Ratnasambhava|qid=Q855742|lt=Ratnasambhava|1=Ratnasambhava}}.]] The Wisdom Kings (Vidyârâjas) were initially divinities of Esoteric Buddhism but were then later adopted by Japanese Buddhism as a whole. These Gods are equipped with superior knowledge and power that give them influence on internal and external reality. These Kings became the object of personification, either peaceful in the case of female personifications, and wrathful in the case of male personifications. Their aggressivity expresses their will to get rid of negative forces in devotees and in the world. They are therefore an expression of the Buddha's compassion for all beings. ====Five Wisdom Kings==== {{main|Five Wisdom Kings}} [[File:Wisdom kings.jpg|thumb|left|[[:en:Five Wisdom Kings|Five Wisdom Kings]]. Front left is a protective deity: the [[Four Heavenly Kings|Heavenly King]] [[:en:Zōchō|Zōchō]], and back left another one: Heavenly King [[:en:Kōmoku|Kōmoku]].]] The [[:en:Five Wisdom Kings|Five Wisdom Kings]] (五大明王) are emanations of the Buddhas and protect them. They are usually represented as violent beings. They represent the ambivalent in nature, and seem to derive from ancient {{ill|Yaksha|en|Yaksha|qid=Q6011093|lt=Yaksa|1=Yaksha}} and [[:en:Vedic Brahmanism|Brahmanical]] tradition.''Tantric Buddhism and altered states of consciousness'' Louise Child p.109 [https://books.google.com/books?id=zOuxJqF2l9AC&pg=PA109] {|border="0" cellspacing="5" | |align = "center"|{{ill|Vajrayakṣa|en|Vajrayakṣa|qid=Q11646516|lt=Kongō-Yasha|1=Vajrayakṣa}} (north) | |- |align = "center"|[[:en:Daiitoku|Daiitoku]] (west) |align = "center"|[[:en:Fudō-Myō|Fudō-Myō]]
(principal deity) |align = "center"| {{ill|Trailokyavijaya|en|Trailokyavijaya|qid=Q532825|lt=Gosanze|1=Trailokyavijaya}} (east) |- | |align = "center"|[[:en:Gundari|Gundari]] (south) | |} Beyond the five principal kings, numerous other [[:en:Wisdom Kings|Wisdom Kings]] exist with a great variety of roles. ====Other Wisdom Kings==== Many more [[:en:Wisdom Kings|Wisdom Kings]] also exist with numerous functions. In general, the Wisdom Kings are viewed as the guardians of the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas. File:Ususama.jpg|[[:en:Ususama|Ususama]]. Image:Bato Kannon painting.jpg|{{ill|Hayagriva|en|Hayagriva|qid=Q1483349|lt=Hayagriva|1=Hayagriva}}. File:Mahamayuri statue.jpg|[[Mahamayuri]]. ===Level 4: Heavenly deities (Ten-bu)=== {{main|Deva (Buddhism)}} [[File:Jikoku Dhrtarashtra.jpg|thumb|The [[Four Heavenly Kings|Heavenly King]] {{ill|Dhṛtarāṣṭra|en|Dhṛtarāṣṭra|qid=Q1188767|lt=Dhṛtarāṣṭra|1=Dhṛtarāṣṭra}} (持国天).]] Gods, although benefiting from an exceptional longevity, nevertheless are submitted to the cycle of rebirths, and remain outside of the world of enlightenment and [[Nirvana]]. They are aiming to reach Nirvana eventually, however, and therefore endeavour to help Buddhism and its devotees. According to Buddhist cosmology, adopted from Indian cosmology, the deities live in the [[:en:Trailõkya|Three Worlds]] and are positioned hierarchically according to their position in respect to the cosmic axis of [[:en:Mount Sumeru|Mount Sumeru]]. High above the mountain resides {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|qid=Q11389|lt=Brahma|1=Brahma}}, on the summit reside the Thirty Three Gods with {{ill|Indra|en|Indra|qid=Q128335|lt=Indra|1=Indra}} as their king, at half-height reside the God Kings of the Orient, and at the bottom inferior deities. Numerous deities are included in the Buddhist pantheon. The term ''Ten'' (天) is the equivalent of the Indian ''{{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}}'' and designated the higher divinities from the [[Four Heavenly Kings]] up. The term ''Jin'' (神) designated lower-level deities. The [[Four Heavenly Kings]] are an important part of these deities. File:Zouchou.jpg|The [[Four Heavenly Kings|Heavenly King]] [[:en:Zōchō|Zōchō]]. File:Bonten_Brahma.jpg|Bonten (梵天)/ {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|qid=Q11389|lt=Brahma|1=Brahma}}. File:Hachi_Dairyuuoo_Nagaraja.jpg|King [[Nanda (half-brother of Buddha)|Nanda]], one of the Hachi Dairyūō (八大竜王)/ [[Nagaraja]]. File:Marishiten_Marici.jpg|{{ill|Marici (Buddhism)|en|Marici (Buddhism)|qid=Q3273446|lt=Marici (Buddhism)|1=Marici (Buddhism)}} (摩利支天)/ Marici. File:Ugajin_masculine_form.jpg|[[Ugajin]] (宇賀神), masculine form. File:Ugajin_feminine_form.jpg|[[Ugajin]] (宇賀神), feminine form. Incomplete list of {{ill|Mikkyō|en|Mikkyō|qid=Q336102|lt=Mikkyō|1=Mikkyō}} devas which were originally Indian {{ill|Hindu deities|en|Hindu deities|qid=Q979507|lt=Hindu deities|1=Hindu deities}}: * {{ill|Agni|en|Agni|qid=Q394867|lt=Agni|1=Agni}} (Katen {{lang|ja|火天}}) – '''Lord of Fire'''; Guardian of the South East * [[:en:Brahmā|Brahmā]] (Bonten {{lang|ja|梵天}}) – '''Lord of the Heavens'''; Guardian of the Heavens (upward direction) * {{ill|Chandra|en|Chandra|qid=Q1061795|lt=Chandra|1=Chandra}} (Gatten {{lang|ja|月天}}) – '''Lord of the Moon''' * {{ill|Indra|en|Indra|qid=Q128335|lt=Indra|1=Indra}} (Taishakuten {{lang|ja|帝釈天}}) – '''Lord of the {{ill|Trāyastriṃśa|en|Trāyastriṃśa|qid=Q1360289|lt=Trāyastriṃśa|1=Trāyastriṃśa}} Heaven and The Thirty Three Devas'''; Guardian of the East * [[Prthivi]] or [[:en:Bhūmī-Devī|Bhūmī-Devī]] (Jiten {{lang|ja|地天}}) – '''Lord of the Earth'''; Guardian of the Earth (downward direction) * {{ill|Rakshasa|en|Rakshasa|qid=Q1049467|lt=Rakshasa|1=Rakshasa}} (Rasetsuten {{lang|ja|羅刹天}}) – '''Lord of Demons'''; Guardian of the South West (converted Buddhist rakshasas) * {{ill|Shiva|en|Shiva|qid=Q11378|lt=Shiva|1=Shiva}} or [[:en:Maheshvara|Maheshvara]] (Daijizaiten {{lang|ja|大自在天}} or Ishanaten {{lang|ja|伊舎那天}}) – '''Lord of The {{ill|Desire realm|en|Desire realm|qid=Q4447630|lt=Desire Realms|1=Desire realm}}'''; Guardian of the North East * [[:en:Sūrya|Sūrya]] (Nitten {{lang|ja|日天}}) – '''Lord of the Sun''' * {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|qid=Q866315|lt=Vaiśravaṇa|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} (Bishamonten {{lang|ja|毘沙門天}} or Tamonten {{lang|ja|多聞天}}) – '''Lord of Wealth'''; Guardian of the North * [[:en:Varuṇa|Varuṇa]] (Suiten {{lang|ja|水天}}) – '''Lord of Water'''; Guardian of the West * [[:en:Vāyu|Vāyu]] (Fūten {{lang|ja|風天}})* '''Lord of Wind'''; Guardian of the North West * {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|qid=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}} (Emmaten {{lang|ja|焔魔天}}) – '''Lord of the Underworld'''; Guardian of the South * {{ill|Kangiten|en|Kangiten|qid=Q11545315|lt=Kangiten|1=Kangiten}} (歓喜天) / {{ill|Ganesha|en|Ganesha|qid=Q1579|lt=Ganesha|1=Ganesha}} * [[Benzaiten]] (弁財天) / [[Saraswati]] * [[Kisshōten]] (吉祥天) / {{ill|Lakshmi|en|Lakshmi|qid=Q131443|lt=Lakshmi|1=Lakshmi}} * Bichūten (毘紐天) / {{ill|Vishnu|en|Vishnu|qid=Q11380|lt=Vishnu|1=Vishnu}} * [[Daikokuten]] (大黒天) / {{ill|Mahakala|en|Mahakala|qid=Q922824|lt=Mahakala|1=Mahakala}} *Umahi (烏摩妃) / {{ill|Parvati|en|Parvati|qid=Q170485|lt=Uma|1=Parvati}} *Kumaraten (鳩摩羅天) / [[Murugan|Kumara]] *Naraenten (那羅延天) / {{ill|Narayana|en|Narayana|qid=Q746495|lt=Narayana|1=Narayana}} *Rago (羅睺) / {{ill|Rahu|en|Rahu|qid=Q950298|lt=Rahu|1=Rahu}} *Izanaten (伊舎那天) / {{ill|Ishana|en|Ishana|qid=Q608366|lt=Ishana|1=Ishana}} * [[:en:Yama (Buddhism and Chinese mythology)|Enma]] (閻魔) / {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|qid=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}} ===Level 5: Circumstantial appearances (Suijakushin)=== {{main|List of Japanese deities|Shintō|Gongen|Shinbutsu shūgō}} Although divinities are considered to be subjects to the law of impermanence, Buddhism nevertheless considers that men should place themselves under their protection. When Buddhism entered Japan in the 6th century numerous [[Shinto|Shintō]] divinities (''[[kami]]'') were also present in the Japanese islands, although they had no {{ill|Iconography|en|Iconography|qid=Q208145|lt=Iconography|1=Iconography}}. The ''shuijakushin'' Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category is specific to Japan and provides for the incorporation into Buddhism of these Shintō ''kami''. The Buddhist term "[[Gongen]]" 権現 or "{{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|qid=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}}" (meaning the capability of the Buddhas and Bodhisattvas to change their appearance to that of a Japanese ''kami'' to facilitate conversion of the Japanese) thus came into use in relation to these gods. Shintō deities came to be considered as local appearances in disguise of foreign Buddhas and Bodhisattvas ({{nihongo|''suijakushin''|垂迹神|circumstantial appearance gods}}).{{cite book|title=Butsuzō no Mikata Handobukku|year=2008|publisher=Ikeda Shoten|location=[[Tokyo]]|isbn=978-4-262-15695-8|editor=Ishii, Ayako|page=120|language=ja}} Thus numerous [[Shinto]] figures have been absorbed as Buddhist deities. This was also sometimes reciprocal, as in the case of Buddhist [[Benzaiten]] and Shinto ''kami'' [[Ugajin]]. This [[Shinbutsu-shūgō|syncretism]] was officially abolished by the establishment of the {{ill|Emperor Meiji|en|Emperor Meiji|qid=Q174231|lt=Emperor Meiji|1=Emperor Meiji}} in 1868 with the ''[[Shinbutsu bunri|Shinto and Buddhism Separation Order]]'' (神仏分離令, also 神仏混淆禁止 ''Shinbutsu Konkō Kinshi''). The Six Kannon are a group of deity sculptures that were originally placed together in the temple at Daihoonji.{{Citation|last=Fowler|first=Sherry D.|chapter=Painting the Six Kannon|date=2016-11-30|publisher=University of Hawai'i Press|isbn=9780824856229|doi=10.21313/hawaii/9780824856229.003.0006|title=Accounts and Images of Six Kannon in Japan}} The sculptures were made by the sculptor Jōkei in 1224. The idea of grouping statues together is popular in Buddhism because it is said that it increases the power of the deities when they are shown as a group. However, it is popular for the Six Kannon to be enshrined at temples throughout Japan individually as well. The group of six consists of [[:en:Shō Kannon|Shō Kannon]], {{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|en|Thousand-armed Kannon|qid=Q10904910|lt=Thousand-armed Kannon|1=Thousand-armed Kannon}}, Horse-headed Kannon, Eleven-headed Kannon, Juntei Kannon, and Nyoirin Kannon. These Six Kannon although alike, have distinct attributes which set them apart from one another. File:Atago Gongen.jpg|Circumstantial appearances of {{ill|Mount Atago|en|Mount Atago|qid=Q2975204|lt=Mount Atago|1=Mount Atago}} (愛宕権現), in the shape of General {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|qid=Q604687|lt=Kṣitigarbha|1=Kṣitigarbha}}. File:Konpira Daigongen.jpg|[[:en:Kompira|Kompira]] Daigongen (金毘羅大権現), divinity of the [[Seto Inland Sea|Inland Sea]] and ships. File:Sambou Koujin Fire Divinity.jpg|{{ill|Kōjin|en|Kōjin|qid=Q6455190|lt=Sambō|1=Kōjin}} Kōjin (三宝荒神), the Fire Divinity. Uses the power of fire for the Buddhist cause. File:Zaou Gongen Circumstancial appearance of Mount Yoshino.jpg|[[Zaō Gongen]] (蔵王権現), circumstantial appearance of {{ill|Mount Yoshino|en|Mount Yoshino|qid=Q1036938|lt=Mount Yoshino|1=Mount Yoshino}}. ===Level 6: Religious masters (''Kōsō''・''Soshi'')=== [[File:Daruma Daishi Bodhidharma.jpg|thumb|"Daruma Daishi" {{ill|Bodhidharma|en|Bodhidharma|qid=Q187172|lt=Bodhidharma|1=Bodhidharma}}, the founder of {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}.]] Buddhism has also created an iconography for the saint men who assisted to its diffusion. These are historical beings, although some legendary elements can be attached to them. Some, such as [[:en:Kōbō-Daishi|Kōbō-Daishi]], the founder of {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon Buddhism|1=Shingon Buddhism}}, are the subject of a devotion equivalent to that of the Buddhas or Bodhisattvas. Some have also acquired the qualities of protective spirits, such as ''{{ill|Battabara|en|Battabara|qid=Q16977638|lt=Battabara|1=Bhadrapāla}}'' protector of the baths, or ''[[:en:Fudaishi|Fudaishi]]'', protector of monastical libraries. The list of these religious masters consists of men from the "Three Countries" where Buddhism was born and then prospered along the {{ill|Silk Road|en|Silk Road|qid=Q36288|lt=Silk Road|1=Silk Road}}: {{ill|India|en|India|qid=Q668|lt=India|1=India}}, {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|qid=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats|1=Sixteen Arhats}}, saint men who were predecessors or disciples of the Buddha, are also part of this Category:100 oldest pages]][[Category. File:Shotoku_Taishi.jpg|{{ill|Prince Shōtoku|en|Prince Shōtoku|qid=Q263972|lt=Prince Shōtoku|1=Prince Shōtoku}}. File:Kobo Daishi.jpg|Master [[:en:Kōbō Daishi|Kōbō Daishi]] founder of [[Shingon]] Buddhism. File:Ingada Sonja.jpg|[[:en:Ingada sonja|Ingada sonja]], one of the {{ill|Sixteen Arhats|en|Sixteen Arhats|qid=Q3477968|lt=Sixteen Arhats|1=Sixteen Arhats}}. File:Battabara sonja.jpg|{{ill|Battabara|en|Battabara|qid=Q16977638|lt=Battabara sonja|1=Bhadrapāla}}, 跋陀婆羅尊者, protector of the baths. File:Fu-Daishi.jpg|[[:en:Fudaishi|Fudaishi]], 傅大士, an originator of {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} and {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}, also invented rotating libraries for prayers. File:Nichiren Shounin.jpg|[[Nichiren Shōnin]], Nichiren聖人, founder of the {{ill|Nichiren Buddhism|en|Nichiren Buddhism|qid=Q871371|lt=Nichiren Buddhism|1=Nichiren Buddhism}}. ==Eight Legions ({{lang-ja|八部衆}}, ''Hachi Bushū'')== {{Further|Eight Legions}} In {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}}, these classes of beings are called the '''Aṣṭagatyaḥ''' or the '''Aṣṭauparṣadaḥ'''. # [[Japanese Buddhist pantheon#Level 4: Heavenly deities (Ten-bu)|Tenbu]] ({{lang-ja|天部}}) / {{ill|Deva (Buddhism)|en|Deva (Buddhism)|qid=Q4089509|lt=Deva|1=Deva (Buddhism)}} # {{ill|Japanese dragon|en|Japanese dragon|qid=Q2366503|lt=Ryū|1=Japanese dragon}} ({{lang-ja|龍}}) / [[Nāga|Naga]] # Yasha ({{lang-ja|夜叉}}) / {{ill|Yaksha|en|Yaksha|qid=Q6011093|lt=Yaksha|1=Yaksha}} # Kendatsuba ({{lang-ja|乾闥婆}}) / {{ill|Gandharva|en|Gandharva|qid=Q1291498|lt=Gandharva|1=Gandharva}} # Ashura ({{lang-ja|阿修羅}}) / [[Asura (Buddhism)|Asura]] # {{ill|Karura|en|Karura|qid=Q82198|lt=Karura|1=Karura}} ({{lang-ja|迦楼羅}}) / {{ill|Garuda|en|Garuda|qid=Q188676|lt=Garuda|1=Garuda}} # Kinnara ({{lang-ja|緊那羅}}) / {{ill|Kinnara|en|Kinnara|qid=Q1425311|lt=Kinnara|1=Kinnara}} # Magoraga ({{lang-ja|摩睺羅伽}}) / {{ill|Mahoraga|en|Mahoraga|qid=Q11078471|lt=Mahoraga|1=Mahoraga}}{{Cite web|url=http://www.onmarkproductions.com/html/hachi-bushu.shtml#karura|title = Hachi Bushu - the Eight Legions, Buddhist Protectors, Japanese Buddhism Photo Dictionary}} == See also == * [[:en:Buddhism in East Asia|Buddhism in East Asia]] * {{ill|Buddhist deities|en|Buddhist deities|qid=Q65122124|lt=Buddhist deities|1=Buddhist deities}} * {{ill|Dharmapala|en|Dharmapala|qid=Q1207630|lt=Dharmapala|1=Dharmapala}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} {{Buddhist Pantheon}} {{Buddhism topics}} [[en:Japanese Buddhist pantheon]] [[fa:پانتئون بودایی ژاپن]] [[fr:Panthéon bouddhiste japonais]] [[id:Panteon Buddhis Jepang]] [[ja:日本の仏尊]] [[lt:Budizmo mitologijos sąvokos]] {{wikidata link|Q6158131}} [[Category:Buddhism in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese deities]] [[Category:Vajrayana deities]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
